> Why ``furl.set`` call ``self.query.load`` not ``self.query.set``?

Because the scope of `furl.set()`'s parameters and `furl.query.set()`'s
parameters are different: `furl.set()` operates at the scope of the entire URL
while `furl.query.set()` operates at the scope of the query.

`furl.set(query=...)`'s `query` parameter represents the entire query of a URL,
hence it calls `Query.load()` instead of `Query.set()`. In English,
`furl.set(query=...)` translates to

> Set the query of the URL to the provided value.

For example, one can also provide an entire query string to `furl.set()`, too:

```python
>>> from furl import furl
>>> f = furl('http://www.google.com/')
>>> f.set(query='a=1&b=2')
furl('http://www.google.com/?a=1&b=2')
>>> f.args
omdict1D([('a', '1'), ('b', '2')])
```

On the other hand, `Query.set(mapping=...)`'s `mapping` paramter is a key:value
mapping of key:value pairs to adopt. In English, this translates to

> Set the keys and values of the Query to those in the provided mapping.

I agree. Two reasons:
1) Differences of world views of the developers brings numerous
accessories with the frameworks with quite different api's .
Although this is a 'problem', it is NOT serious as the following one:

2) Choices made for main execution logic affects the
main structure. That is, they change the way async functionality
is exposed to outside world. This mix consists of coroutines,
generators, even sync functions. It determines the core api.

(1) 'may' be resolved with some consensus. May be not since
it is also related but (2) but with some indirection.

But (2) is not an easy one. Anyone who wants to deal with
the problem should have sharp view about the very 'core' ingredients
and their differences between framework.

Ok, I think I can see what you're doing. Let me know if I understand you
correctly or not.

Basically, you want some asynchronous functions (including queue.put) to
have a synchronous version that can be called with the same name and
signature (without the await) and you added the @awaitable decorator for
this reason.

To determine which version is being called, you basically looked at the
call stack to see if the calling function was async or not... which was
failing when a comprehension was used in an async context.

To fix that, you factored out that looking at the call stack in a function
that calls itself recursively higher on the stack whenever it notices the
calling context is a comprehension.

The only thing I don't understand is why _is_coroutine calls itself with
level+2 (instead of level+1). I would have said level+1 is the function
into which the comprehension is nested, and level+2 the function that
called it. What am I missing?

@scelen I've added support for Dynamic Content.

This doesn't work for secure websockets as these need to be configured on the server-side. This will require generation of secure certificates for the domain that you're running on (ie localhost).

In essence users wanting to use SSL will need to generate a certificate and add it to the configuration for the Mopidy and Pusher websockets to use. I would prefer to have a self-signed certificate but out of the box, self-signed certificates are denied by [most] browsers.

This would ultimately render Spotmop unusable out of the box, and for the bulk of users SSL is not required. So further work is required to make it optional. I do think it would be good practice to facilitate it for users who require it though.

I've moved this into a new branch (`feature/secure`) to contain any further work on this without interrupting the main development flow.

This is a question to @jedisct1 regarding usage of libsodium, asking here as I can't see how to contact privately. I want to use libsodium to implement public key authentication for a private HTTP API and have not found any documentation on the best way to do this.

Here is my process flow at present:

Client has private key, server has clients public key.
Client requests authentication from server.
Server generates and stores a nonce and sends to client (as in SQRL).
Client signs nonce with private key.
Server verifies signature, and that nonce equals one it has generated.

I think this is secure, however it does open the server to a possible resource exhaustion attack. A malicious entity could send a huge number of requests to the request auth endpoint, allocating a huge number of nonces. That could be a problem even if they are made to expire.

Is it possible to do this without needing to store nonces on the server?

I ran into this a while ago. I think the problem is that one of the Windows backends, EncryptedKeyring has a fixed priority of .8 regardless of whether or not it's supported.

This priority is higher than the file based keyrings. If, for whatever reason, you can't use any platform specific keyring in *NIX then the Windows keyring will be used first.

[See here](https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/src/3827c087ed2710a912a4e202c49e3639472fbcbd/keyring/backends/Windows.py?at=default#cl-38)

@craffel I haven't looked at the code (or the mirex code) so I'm not sure if this is relevant, but here goes:

I imagine that if the estimated key is a perfect fifth above the true key then you get 0.5 because they are highly related (tonally). The [circle of fifths](https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circle_of_fifths) is not symmetric: the fifth of C is G, but the fifth of G is D. So if the true key is a C, estimating a G (a fifth) is closely related, but estimating a perfect fifth down (an F) is not as tonally related (F is the 4th for C, not the 5th) and hence not rewarded at all.

Estimating a G *below* the C (ie a perfect 4th down) should still get rewarded, but since this is key estimation I imagine this is octave agnostic right? Hence you only have to look for a fifth "up" (in reality its a chroma circle, so there is no up and down)

Do you mean the validation check at https://github.com/dgilland/pushjack/blob/c85f47c085626fed44d885eadc58b162ddeda45a/pushjack/apns.py#L218 or somewhere else?

That token validation is checking only on the format of the token (https://github.com/dgilland/pushjack/blob/c85f47c085626fed44d885eadc58b162ddeda45a/pushjack/apns.py#L733) which basically just checks that it's valid HEX and has the expected length. It doesn't check whether the token actually exists within the APNS network.

Generally, if a token fails this check, there's something fundamentally wrong with the token (wrong length or contains non-HEX characters). This usually indicates that the token wasn't saved correctly or something modified it to make it unusable.

Hey @prijutme4ty!

Interesting addition; I'll have to spend some more time looking at it to make a decision.

I'll have to spend more time looking at the missing `:last` selectors too. The reason there is a `:last` selector is used is in cases where multiple rows are included in the `thead`. Lets say we have this markup:

```html
<thead>
  <tr>
    <th class="sorter-false" rowspan="2">Index</th>
    <th class="sorter-false" colspan="3">Groups</th>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <th class="sorter-date">Group 1</th>
    <th class="sorter-url">Group 2</th>
    <th class="sorter-digit">Group 3</th>
  </tr>
</thead>
```

The `config.$headers` option will include ALL of the cells, but the column indexing won't match. So the `$.tablesorter.computeColumnIndex` function calculates the correct column indexes for the HTML markup and adds a `data-column="#"` attribute.

So, in the case above, the markup will end up looking like this:

```html
<thead>
  <tr>
    <th class="sorter-false" rowspan="2" data-column="0">Index</th>
    <th class="sorter-false" colspan="3" data-column="1">Groups</th>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <th data-column="1" class="sorter-date">Group 1</th>
    <th data-column="2" class="sorter-url">Group 2</th>
    <th data-column="3" class="sorter-digit">Group 3</th>
  </tr>
</thead>
```

So now if I need to get the parser for the second column, I need to get it from the last cell with `data-column="1"`. I hope that makes sense.

The `widget-uitheme` uses `$headers` for all headers, including cloned sticky and scroller headers. The `$h` variable refers to the actual table header; but it looks like in this case it basically is the same as `$headers` and could likely be combined. Again, I'll look into this when I get more time.

I'm curious about the use case, are you processing existing text or is this for active user input relative to the current time? There may be some consideration of the current time required... if it's 5am now and I write "breakfast at 6" I probably mean 6am but at noon "yoga at 6" probably means 6pm. However, for example if your implementation is a diary rather than a task scheduler the user will generally be speaking about the past and you may want this logic to work differently. 

Consider a similar problem at a yearly granularity. In November 2016 if I reference "January 12" am I referring to January 2016 or 2017? It depends on the context and different implementations will have different answers for that depending on whether they normally expect past or future date references. I believe that the default is to assume the next occurrence, so in this example January 12, 2017. 

Extrapolating that to the time of day case, is it desirable for a setting to control whether "meet at 8:30" means 8:30am when it is before that time otherwise it means 8:30pm? When it is after 8:30PM does "8:30" refer to 8:30am of the following day?

`BufferedReader` is suitable when you want to do other blocking operations in your application and only process messages from time to time. That's what I was trying to exemplify with `time.sleep`. While your application is doing something else, the Notifier and BufferedReader will process the receive buffer for you.

In your case it might not make much of a difference since I don't see any blocking operations except maybe for some file writing? You still need to have enough CPU resources (on a single core since threads run on the same core) to both receive messages and process them.

To bring [FakeNet-NG](github.com/fireeye/flare-fakenet-ng) on Linux, I used a cython wrapper for `libnetfilter_queue` [python-netfilterqueue](https://github.com/kti/python-netfilterqueue). It has two issues, though:
1. Hard-coded 4,096-byte buffer in the release version only admits 4,012-byte datagrams after accounting for overhead, meaning that you will can miss data on some interfaces with high maximum transmittal units (MTUs) such as `lo` which is often greater than 4k. Recompiling is an option but then your users can't use the normal Python package management facilities to get `python-netfilterqueue` installed automatically (e.g. via `setup.py`); and,
2. It doesn't accommodate multi-threaded applications in and of itself, but you can work around this using a [socket timeout](https://github.com/fireeye/flare-fakenet-ng/blob/master/fakenet/diverters/linutil.py#L141) and a [loop](https://github.com/fireeye/flare-fakenet-ng/blob/master/fakenet/diverters/linutil.py#L157)

The best resource for getting acquainted with the typical usage like this is the [SikuliX documentation](http://sikulix-2014.readthedocs.org/en/latest/index.html), as Lackey is intended to be a drop-in replacement.

First, DebugMaster is the uninstantiated class; what you're looking for is the Debug object:

    from lackey import *
    a = "C:\go.log"
    Debug.setLogFile(a)

For more details, check out the [log and debug messages documentation](http://sikulix-2014.readthedocs.io/en/latest/scripting.html?highlight=debug#debuglog).

Second, you absolutely can set the level of confidence for finding a pattern. This can be configured globally by changing `Settings.MinSimilarity`, or individually by passing a Pattern object instead of just the path.

By default, `Settings.MinSimilarity` is `0.7`. If you don't want to change this globally, you can set it individually by calling the pattern's `similar()` method:

    Settings.MinSimilarity = 0.8
    find("image_to_find.png") # Will only find matches with confidence 0.8 or greater
    find(Pattern("image_to_find.png").similar(0.9)) # Will only find matches with confidence 0.9 or greater

For more details, check out the [Pattern documentation](http://sikulix-2014.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pattern.html).

Hi,

Thanks for the explanation, but I'm not sure it completely makes sense here :). In our case we update a large array that takes say 10s. You can run it in one go and it will block the browser for 10s, or you can do 0.1% of it, repaint, then do the 99.9% in another go and it will still block the browser for 9.59s:

I think it's:

```
+--------+
| Render |
+--------+
    |
+---------------+
| block for 10s |
+---------------+
    |
+--------+
| Render |
+--------+
    |
```

versus:

```
+--------+
| Render |
+--------+
    |
+---------------+
| block for 0.1s |
+---------------+
    |
+--------+
| Render |
+--------+
    |
+-----------------+
| block for 9.59s |
+-----------------+
    |
+--------+
| Render |
+--------+
    |
```

You can split the work in two batches, but it takes the same time and will still block the browser. We were in fact able to reproduce that in a simple case without AngularJS (pure JS). Regardless if we put the for loop inside or outside a `setTimeout()` the browser would still get blocked, which I think makes sense.

What we observe here is that updating the large array in a `$timeout()` doesn't block the UI at all anymore, which we have trouble explaining. We've timed the loop when inside and outside the `$timeout` and it takes the same time (~4ms). It seems what is slow is repainting somehow, or something that happens *after* the array has been updated.
* When the loop is outside a timeout, it runs in 4ms but then the UI blocks for 10s
* When the loop is inside a timeout, it still runs in 4ms but then the UI doesn't block

That's what we can't explain, and our concern is that it looks a bit hacky so we're afraid we're missing something here that will cause us grief later.

Possible leads:
* Something to do with Angular scope digesting which would be a lot faster (or happen less frequently?) when inside a timeout?
* Or something to do with rendering, where it behaves differently when it's in a single loop vs. separated in two loops with the big one being inside a timeout.

Hi Daniel,

I noticed this issue as well recently, wrote it down as a "note to self" but hadn't created a public issue for it yet. Thanks for reporting this! This change in semantics of Python 2 versus Python 3 actually surprised me because it breaks certain use cases of the well known Python [decorate-sort-undecorate](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting#The_Old_Way_Using_Decorate-Sort-Undecorate) idiom.

FYI: Your fix changes the semantics so it no longer uses natural order sorting. Whether that's a serious problem for you is another topic, basically it depends on the filenames you use and whether they contain zero padded numbers.

I'll see how I can fix this and report back here soon.

Looks good to me, thanks!  :)

Hi Bruno,

Any news on this ?

OK, so I've had a look around too (more widely on native and web UIs) and:

1. Cursor keys on radio buttons don't just move the focus, they also change the selected option.  This is what asciimatics already does and so I don't propose to change this.
2. Tabbing into a radio button list just moves the focus to the current selected option in the list - which is what asciimatics already does.  Again no change.

I think that's what you said midnight commander also did, so I think we're clean here.  Please shout if I've missed something.

Looking at multi-line edit, all the examples I've found also limit you to the the text area once you move the focus into it.  The only way out is typically with a tab.  I'm not sure that this is expected navigation.  Do you have examples of other systems that allow you to drop straight out of a multi-line edit using the up and down keys?  I think it's possible to add this feature, but given how prevalent the existing function is, I wouldn't want to change the default for other users without a good precedent.

That leaves the navigation from Layout to Layout.  This is certainly the area I'm least satisfied with, but I've yet to find anything that does any better.  A quick play on bootstrap forms shows that use of grids pretty much disables any cursor key navigation.  More complex native forms quickly force you to tab round to get anywhere.  That said, if there's no great precedent for handling this, beyond using the TAB key, maybe I should just do what is easy and allow up/down keys to move between Layouts?

At least there should be documentation what is correct way to use security extension when using flask factor pattern. Many of the extensions are used like this:

```
extension = Extension()
def create_app(config_filename):
    app = Flask(__name__)
    extension.init_app(app)
```

With Flask-Security there will be infinite loop with __getattr__ when using this pattern.

I was able to get it working this way:

```
security = None
def create_app(config_filename):
    global security
    app = Flask(__name__)
    security = Security(app)
```

@RantyDave @graingert What matters more is absolutely a matter of opinion. *However*, it is unquestionable that the WHATWG considers two or more slashes after the scheme equivalent to just two, and so if any Python project changes to using the WHATWG view of a URL then the "triple-slash" scheme will fall over in a heap.

Note also that this happens with Requests:

```python
>>> r = requests.get('http+unix:///var/run/docker.sock/version')
requests.exceptions.InvalidURL: Invalid URL u'http+unix:///var/run/docker.sock/version': No host supplied
```

Specifically, if Requests thinks your scheme is a HTTP scheme then it wants a conforming URL, and there is no conforming URL that a user can provide that does not have a host part. So that's not going to fly.

That might not be what we want.

I played around with `openssl genrsa` a bit more. It seems that the passphrase (which can be specified in a number of ways: stdin, or a file, or the command line, or an env variable) can be any sequence of bytes. Not only can the passphrase be ASCII, but it can also be a utf-8 encoded non-ASCII string, or a latin-1 encoded non-ASCII string, etc. If the passphrase happens to be non-ASCII, and wasn't encoded with utf-8, then we won't be able to auto-encode to `bytes`.

No matter what, we should enhance the documentation to say that `bytes` is required. Beyond that, our options are:
1) Do nothing else.
2) Raise a `TypeError` with a helpful message, if the passphrase is not `bytes` (or `str` on python 2).
3) If the passphrase is `unicode`, attempt to encode to `bytes` with encoding `ascii`. If that fails, raise our own `TypeError` with a helpful message.
4) If the passphrase is `unicode`, attempt to encode to `bytes` with encoding `utf-8`. If that fails, raise our own `TypeError` with a helpful message.

3) Would be a nice thing to do, for the benefit of those out there (ourselves included!) that use `unicode_literals`. I could maybe be convinced to do 4), but I lean against it. I think I'd prefer the user do their own encoding: they know best what their passphrase is.

Re @Jeff-Meadows 
> Should we change the type from str to six.binary_type?
>
> Most of our docstrings refer to the Python 2 types (eg unicode). Should we overhaul this?

I don't think changing `unicode` is necessary at this time. We talked about this a while ago, and I think we decided that `unicode` was clear enough, even if it is technically nonsensical on Python 3. We could overhaul this if we wanted to, but it's probably not necessary in response to this issue.

Given that we're using `unicode` to mean the Python text type (where `unicode` doesn't exist on Python 3, but is actually `str`), I think the analogous thing to do here would be to use `bytes` to mean the Python byte string type (where `bytes` doesn't exist on Python 2, but is actually `str`). And then we'd use `str` in the circumstances where the function requires a native string (`str` on both Python 2 and 3).

@formath you bring up a good point. Large indices is definitely a feature we want to support in the long-term. We might want to open a separate issue and discuss this. 

First of all, we do plan to add sparse support for Embedding op, where the weight can be in [row_sparse](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/versions/master/api/python/ndarray/sparse.html) format, and the gradient for the weight should be generated in row_sparse format, too. I am currently working on code refactoring and documentations so this sparse operator is not implemented yet. 

Regarding large indices up to 64 bits, this requires the first task @piiswrong brought up regarding int types in the C API, and the `Kernel::Launch` API in the backend uses 32-bit int instead of 64-bit, which is problematic for many operators which operate on ndarrays of large shape. So the scope is bigger than just the embedding op and definitely, it takes some more time to resolve. 

Are you working on any industrial scale dataset? Two ways to circumvent the 64-bit hashed-index problem in my mind:
1.  rehashing the indices into around 23 or 24 bit to reduce the dimensionality, which doesn't hurt much as claimed by [some paper](https://arxiv.org/pdf/0902.2206.pdf), and doesn't cause the operator to break in MXNet. 
2.  preprocessing the dataset to find out the number of unique features and map them to continuous indices instead.

@jgao54 do you want to try this patch out and verify that GCS works?

In order to call your endpoint the approach is something like
```python
client = ...
response = client.RESOURCE.OPERATION(context="a string).result(timeout=1)
```

* RESOURCE is defined according to the swagger tag associated with the operation or the "first part" of your path ([code reference](https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/master/bravado_core/resource.py#L68)). In your case you should have no tags defined so it will be ``wp``
* OPERATION is defined by ``operationId`` attribute if present otherwise by ``<HTTP_VERB>_<PATH_with_no_underscores>`` ([code reference](https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/master/bravado_core/operation.py#L13)). In your case you should have ``get_wp_v2_posts``

My suggestion would be to structure a bit more your specs in order to define ``tags`` and ``operationId``s (they are not required fields for Swagger but allows to to have a better experience with tools and to "classify" endpoints)
```yaml
basePath: /wp-json
...
tags:
  - name: my_tag
    description: This tag takes care of a certain area of the API
paths:
  /wp/v2/posts:
    get:
      operationId: get_post_v2
      tags:
        - my_tag
      ...
```
By applying those changes you can access your endpoint as
```python
client.my_tag.get_post_v2(context="a string").result()
```

Hey @snopoke, this is great. Some thoughts:

- In the interests of keeping jsonobject small, something like this feels too specialized to be part of the library. I'd be a fan of having it inside commcare-hq.
- The syntax/naming/API feels a little clunky to me, in that it takes a little while to figure out what it's trying to do. The target syntax I'd love would be something like this:
```python
class Penguin(JsonObject):
    animal_name = TypeProperty('penguin')

class Tiger(JsonObject):
    animal_name = TypeProperty('tiger')

class ZooPen(JsonObject):
    # this is reminiscent of languages that have algebraic types, which is kind of what this is
    animal = ObjectProperty(Pinguin | Tiger)
    # alternatively, we could have something like 
    # animal = ObjectProperty(CompoundType(Pinguin, Tiger))
```

(If we come upon a syntax that seems intuitive and generic enough, it might change my mind about not belonging inside jsonobject)

First this is a RTD issue, not a colander issue.

It appears that RTD added PDF generation as a default setting around 4 months ago. Out of all the Pylons Projects, I think only Pyramid supports PDF generation and has done so for years, but the configuration to get it right with RTD is not trivial. Given that HTML generates just fine on RTD, I'm going to close this issue as "won't fix", although anyone who wants to take the time to add PDF generation support at RTD is more than welcome to take it on. The first step would be to get PDF to build locally.

First, some justification as to why `scikit-image.color` is not sufficient

* very heavy dependency (scikit and scipy are overkill and lotsa MB)
* Requires shuffling axis order, not a huge deal but a complexity and an opportunity for hard-to-detect bugs
* slower than our custom Cython code (at least with RGB<->LCH)

> So is the issue here that pinned things become unpinned?

The issue is that complex requirements are treated as if they were unpinned, even if they're pinned.

The `--keep-outdated` logic loops through each dependency that isn't pinned and sets its value to whatever it was before. It doesn't change any values that are pinned because it knows that changing the value in the lockfile for a pinned version could only be a mistake.

Previously, that logic thought that a requirement like `{version = "==2.18.0"}` was unpinned, even though it is clearly pinned. As a result, if the user changed it to `{version = "==2.18.4"}` the resolution process would pick up the correct new version, but the `--keep-outdated` loop would set the lockfile version back to its old value of `2.18.0`.

FWIW, there is another bug lurking in the above, that this PR doesn't fix. If a user has `2.18.0` in their lockfile and changes a requirement from `>=2.18.0` to `>=2.18.4` then the `--keep-outdated` logic will preserve that `2.18.0` in their lockfile, even though it is now invalid. Fixing this would require adding a requirement checking step in the `--keep-outdated` section that checks whether then new requirement is met. I think that should be done in a separate PR, since it's a separate bug (and rarer, given how people write Pipfiles).

I would also like to request this feature for a different reason: writing portable python 2/3 compatible code that can be used on systems with changing python environments.

A lot of scientific compute clusters make use of some sort of `modules` system to allow the install of multiple competing software side-by-side. Running `module load x` alters a suite of environmental variables to make that software run.

This creates an issue for any python code that works natively in both python2 and 3, because if the user installs the software with setuptools for both python 2 and 3, the last used interpreter is hardcoded into the shebang line. When the environment changes and a different version of python is used, those scripts will now fail to run as their parent interpreter cannot function in the new environment.

The easy way around this is to make the shebang line `#!/usr/bin/env python`. Please add an option to the setup.py config to set the interpretter to `env`.

For a full discussion of this issue, please see my stackoverflow question here: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/37874445/override-the-shebang-mangling-in-python-setuptools

> But you would know you overshadowed a test because the test coverage decreased, no? If not then it could be argued said shadowed test is unnecessary.

In that case no because I either added both tests in the same PR or the tests were hitting the C api which doesn't have any code coverage metrics set up. Still, it's also not true in the general case, consider:

```python
def my_func(x, y):
    return x + y

def test_my_func():
    assert my_func(3, 7) == 10

def test_my_func():
    a = my_func(1, float('nan'))
    assert a != a
```

Say I accidentally copy-pasted or auto-completed the second "def test_my_func", when it should have been `test_my_func_nan`. Both tests are necessary and both induce the same line coverage, but I'd only detect this accidental shadowing if I have coverage on the tests themselves.

> And with tests included the coverage percentage does not answer the question of how much of the pkg_resources/setuptools code is covered since this now includes the testsuite... I did try to split and group coverage info with flags on codecov but the results were not as expected!

You can get those actual numbers from the detailed breakdowns - the bulk number is more or less meaningless anyway, what's important is that the number trends towards 100% over time. If someone adds a test to the test suite, I want to be able to look at the diff, see which lines are covered both in their test and in the library itself, and see that every line of their patch was executed as part of the test suite. Excluding tests hurts that in favor of having the arbitrary metric of "library code coverage" be more "accurate", which I think is not a useful goal.

Sorry, the initial summary is not correct. The issue takes place in a "relocatable" virtualenv. If virtualenv is made "relocatable" and some script (e.g. bin/pip or any other) is called using the virtualenv's python binary (bin/python), then sys.prefix is modified twice: in site.py and in activate_this.py.

In regards to sciunto's question, I would describe VisuShrink as having the specific property that as the signal size approaches infinity, the probability of any pure (Gaussian) noise coefficients surviving the threshold approaches zero (eq. 31 of the reference above).

Visually, the images produced will look overly smooth as compared to the BayesShrink algorithm.  There may be some instances in which the VisuShrink result is preferred, but the BayesShrink case is almost always going to give a result with higher MSE, PSNR and mSSIM metrics.

With the `__class__` approach, all tox environments for the branch [I referenced before](  https://github.com/zopefoundation/BTrees/compare/master...NextThought:issue_2_pickle?w=0) are green and pickles are identical.

There's no need for a metaclass to make `isinstance` work out. For reasons having to do with the way `isinstance` is implemented, it's possible to be an instance of two unrelated classes without any subclass customization:

```python
>>> class X(object):
...   pass
...
>>> class Y(object):
...   @property
...   def __class__(self):
...     return X
...
>>> isinstance(Y(), Y)
True
>>> isinstance(Y(), X)
True
```

In `isinstance(inst, Type)`, if the C object's `ob_type` is identical to or a subclass of Type, checking is short-circuited---that's the first case. Otherwise, `inst.__class__` is checked to see if it's a subclass of Type---that's the second case.

Thanks for raising this issue.

**Short answer**
Yes, this is a problem.
You can get around it by running: `python test_fire.py test [1,2] tolist`

**Longer answer**
Since ndarrays are objects and don't fall into any of Fire's special cases, the [help screen for the ndarray is shown](https://github.com/google/python-fire/blob/e76a10c0511ea4485b57543ccb966e7778213279/fire/core.py#L221).

The simplest way to fix this would be to change the way Fire renders ndarrays by adding a [special case here](https://github.com/google/python-fire/blob/e76a10c0511ea4485b57543ccb966e7778213279/fire/core.py#L202).
However, we don't want to add numpy as a dependency of fire, just to be able to check for ndarrays.

So perhaps a better (often requested) option is to allow people to extend / modify the way that Fire serializes it's outputs. We don't have this available yet, but it's a good idea if we can do it well.

For some context, right now we use `name` in two places:
1. [Usage strings](https://github.com/google/python-fire/blob/master/fire/trace.py#L193)
2. [Completion script generation](https://github.com/google/python-fire/blob/master/fire/completion.py#L33)

And there are two times I can think of where you'd maybe (?) want to set `name` explicitly:
1. If the command is commonly called by an alias
2. If the command is commonly called inside of another script w/ a different name (if you want usage/completion to work with that outer script).

It's worth noting here that it's possible for a completion script to do alias resolution, though we don't do it yet.

I also need to think more about what name + usage strings should look like if you call Fire from inside a function that was already called by Fire (as we will likely do in #29).

Small example to reproduce the problem (this is still difficult to reproduce, it depends on the speed of the machine, the network and the speed of the sentry server):

```
from unittest import mock

import aiohttp.resolver

import asyncio
from functools import partial

from raven import Client
from raven import breadcrumbs
from raven_aiohttp import QueuedAioHttpTransport


SENTRY_DSN = ''

# logger names should be exact, ignoring parents does not ignore childs
# ignore_loggers = ('raven.base.Client', 'sentry.errors.uncaught', 'sentry.errors')
# for logger in ignore_loggers:
#     breadcrumbs.ignore_logger(logger)


with mock.patch('aiohttp.resolver.DefaultResolver', aiohttp.resolver.AsyncResolver):
    client = Client(dsn=SENTRY_DSN, transport=partial(QueuedAioHttpTransport, workers=2, qsize=10, verify_ssl=False))

    class KeepMemory:
        def __init__(self, a, b, c):
            self.a = a
            self.b = b
            self.c = c


    async def produce():
        for i in range(1, 10000):
            try:
                big = 1
                a = 'a' * big
                b = 'b' * big + a
                c = 'c' * big + b
                raise Exception(KeepMemory(a, b, c))
            except Exception as e:
                client.captureException()
            await asyncio.sleep(0.001)
            if i % 100 == 0:
                print(i)


    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(produce())
    loop.run_until_complete(client.remote.get_transport().close())


print('waiting 10 seconds to show memory')
import time
time.sleep(10)
```

>Is there any reason we expect the real atmosphere to actually produce a nice Kolmogorov-y thing plus a faint delta function at the center?

Seems unlikely, but I guess nobody could probably tell in reality.  For one, this is in the infinite exposure limit.  For another, real telescopes always cut off power at maxk = 2 pi D / lambda, so aren't capable of producing delta functions.

That said, I think if we were to make a VonKarman profile, given how pervasive the vK power spectrum is, I'd be inclined to just follow the math, even when it's wonky.  It'll get sorted out in the next convolution.  There's *always* an Airy function or something like it waiting to cut the power.

Yes. That's the same behaviour as the issue I mentioned. It's the same one.
Every Gnome app uses Wayland, so that's why it doesn't work on gedit, nautilus, no window, etc.
Firefox (and most other non-gnome programs) are running on Wayland through XWayland, and in that case it will work.
The issue is that Guake uses a global hotkey library made for Xorg, and there is no interfaces in the Wayland specification yet for global hotkeys so that a similar library can be developed for Wayland.
The only workaround as of now is configuring hotkeys on your window manager of choice for opening and closing Guake as specified by [this](https://github.com/Guake/guake/issues/492#issuecomment-119075514) and [this](https://github.com/Guake/guake/issues/492#issuecomment-324165795) comment, the first one explaining the workaround and the second one providing a way to reduce the delay of guake opening and closing when using the provided workaround.
Another workaround is simply ditching Wayland entirely and choosing Gnome on Xorg as your session on GDM or whatever display manager you're using.

The periodic boundary of PPI and other boundary effects are going to be tricky.  The morphology and label functions in SciPy's `ndimage` sub-package do not deal with these issues.  The filter function in `ndimage` do have a **mode** parameter which specified how boundaries are handled which might be helpful to see how those are implemented.  In the region dealiasing routine I addressed the periodic boundary by allowing edges between regions on either side of the boundary.  This is not perfect, the preferred method would be to allow regions to span the boundary, but it seems to work well enough.

I'm thinking a quick and dirty despeckling routine which uses **ndimage.label** and removes 'small' regions would be a good start.  From there some of these more difficult issues can be resolved as needed.

I did some sleuthing and found out why the unexpected success causes a failure in [Python 3][1] but not [Python 2][2].

```python
def wasSuccessful(self):
    return len(self.failures) == len(self.errors) == 0
```

became

```python
def wasSuccessful(self):
    return ((len(self.failures) == len(self.errors) == 0) and
            (not hasattr(self, 'unexpectedSuccesses') or
             len(self.unexpectedSuccesses) == 0))
```

[1]: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.4/Lib/unittest/result.py#L159-L166
[2]: https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Lib/unittest/result.py#L144-L146

The point above still stands - this is a python issue - not a pants issue.  Python simply does not support a package being defined in more than one file system tree.  As a bolt-on, there is python support for namespace packages [1], allowing a single package to be split across several source trees.  Pants may or may not work with namespace package declarations that split 1 package between source and test trees today - I have not tried.

[1] https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/__init__.py#L1

> I'm still unclear on the Jane/Jake scenario though. ...

@benjyw your analysis is correct. Jake and Jane would ping-pong not writing to the cache in that scenario; ie: where they are both working the same target set, testing with `--fast`, doing mods to a subset, getting green and pushing code for the other to pull and iterate on and where there is no intervening entity running a full clean test on that same target set. I'll include a discussion of well supported use cases and less optimal ones in the comments. The gist though is that caching will work best for `--no-fast` and, although its supported for `--fast`, the likelihood of a cache write for a target set matching that used by cache readers is small.

The testing should be done in [`test_build_local_python_distributions.py`](https://github.com/CMLivingston/pants/blob/3eaf931d8dbd55ab1d24df5444014f75bbb72f41/tests/python/pants_test/backend/python/tasks/test_build_local_python_distributions.py#L46) as another synthesized target instead, you can just copy the contents of `test_python_create_platform_specific_distribution()` in that file. Make sure the synthesized target you create has no `python_dist()` native sources, and uses a `ctypes_compatible_cpp_library()`. We should actually probably have two projects, one including just a `*_cpp_library()` and one with just a `*_c_library()`, so that we know each target is activating the platform-specific dist.

```
13:13 < meejah> ...and spreads them out over the bucket-size
```

asyncio is very low-level

For KeyboardInterrupt, there are varied behaviours. Check this issue : https://github.com/python/asyncio/issues/341

It depends on how you trigger `KeyboardInterrupt`. When there is a callback that doesn't return - you need to use the signal to break through that, eg a loop like

```python
while True: 
    pass
```
Adding `asyncio.loop.add_signal_handler` _might_ not work because it wouldn't stop such a loop. 

Otoh, if there's an event loop or a queue running, and you do not want the signal to interrupt that routine, you can use `asyncio.loop.add_signal_handler` .

Another way to do it (naively), would be : 

```python
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()

tasks = asyncio.gather(
        asyncio.async(some_task(second=5)),
        asyncio.async(some_task(seconds=10))
    )

try:
    loop.run_until_complete(tasks)
except KeyboardInterrupt as e:
    print("Caught keyboard interrupt..")
    tasks.cancel()
    loop.run_forever()
    tasks.exception()
finally:
    loop.close()
```

> `web3.exceptions.InvalidAddress: ('Address has an invalid EIP checksum', '0x3db0a82491cca2b89e61db4c1885a8e745c63265')`

Try to get the address from a source that checksums the address. If you're interacting with a contract deployed by someone else, request a checksummed address from them, or look it up on Etherscan.

> My problem is that web3.utils.abi.is_encodable doesn't consider int as representative as bytes array.

Unfortunately, you can lose information when converting back and forth from int to bytes. If you have an abi `bytes` value of `b'\x00\x0a'` and convert that to int, you get 10. Convert that back to `bytes` and you get `b'\x0a'`.

You could maybe make the case that it is reasonable to automatically convert `int` to a `bytesN` type with fixed length, since it can be reliably converted to and from `int`. (your example is fixed length) The subtlety of accepting an `int` for some `bytes` types and not others is likely to cause a lot of confusion though. So I'm hesitant to add it.

> Some kind of validation applies two times and working in different way when try to find method in ABI and when creates a transaction. Isn't?

Right, since functions are polymorphic, we have to look up the appropriate contract function at runtime, based on the types of the arguments. The error message can definitely be improved. #757 is tracking that.

> Maybe could you advise me how to encode automatically a python primitives for any contract method without inspect ABI? I'm sure web3 does it for itself.

I don't understand. Surely you need to know what function you're interacting with, and the types of the arguments, which requires looking at the ABI. In general, web3.py *does* convert from native python types to the correct ABI representation.
Also I want to clarify by deprecating `contract.constructor().deploy`, you mean people should be able to call `contract.constructor().transact()` without the suffix function name to deploy contract?

Hi @danvk , the problem with AlignedSegment is that these objects can live in a context that is independent of a SAM-file. The SAM-file keeps track of chromosome names, while the AlignedSegment contains only an index of the chromosomal identifier (tid). Hence, the only time an AlignedSegment
can be written as a full SAM entry is within the context of a SAM-file (AlignmentFile). Thus, str can never be complete SAM format.
It might be worth adding a json dump to AlignedSegment.

The very first line explains what happened:

```
2015-10-02 06:00:03,844 WARNING elasticsearch log_request_fail:82 POST http://localhost:9200/logstash-2015.10.01/_optimize?max_num_segments=1 [status:N/A request:21600.056s]
```

`request 21600.056s` means that it timed out, which is evident from several other indicators in the logs, e.g. `ReadTimeoutError` and `Read timed out. (read timeout=21600)`.  See [the optimize command documentation regarding timeouts](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/optimize.html#_a_word_about_timeouts) for more information.


This all means that that one, single optimize call took over 6 hours to return, _**but the job continued in the background and completed in spite of the client timeout.**_  

Why was it unable to succeed in the 6 hour default timeout?  This could be for several reasons, including:

1. Indexing is heavy
2. Too few nodes to handle the write load
3. Using slow spinning disks instead of SSDs
4. ???

Options include adding nodes, switching to SSDs, or the cheaper fix of increasing the `--timeout` and `--request_timeout` options _both_ to something larger even than 6 hours (6 x 3600 seconds = 21600).  If your indices are very large, consider optimizing to a larger number segments per shard rather than 1, or the default of 2.

@untergeek 

I am hitting this issue as well.  Being that most people work in daily indices and curate monthly, this unexpected behaviour will probably be encountered by most curator users.    Perhaps we could improve [the documentation](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/time-unit.html) around how to calculate monthly curating?

Looks like there's ~3 misunderstandings on your part here -- I'll highlight them in the docs for you :)

Neither that config, nor that config with `files: ''` should match anything:

From [this](https://pre-commit.com/#filtering-files-with-types):

> `types` and `files` are evaluated with `AND` when filtering. Tags within `types` are also evaluated using `AND`.

That is, if you have `types: [scss, markdown]` you're asking for all files which are **both** markdown _and_ scss.

Additionally, defaults:

From [this](https://pre-commit.com/#pre-commit-configyaml---hooks)

(this is for `.pre-commit-config.yaml`)

> `files` (optional) override the default pattern for files to run on.
> `types` (optional) override the default file types to run on

That is, it'll inherit any value specified in the repository manifest and only if you override it will that change your outcome.

The defaults _are_ as you'd expect.  From [this](https://pre-commit.com/#new-hooks):

> `files`: (optional: default `''`) the pattern of files to run on. new in 0.15.0: now optional.
> `types`: (optional: default `[file]`) list of file types to run on. 

That is, without specifying anything in `.pre-commit-hooks.yaml` or `.pre-commit-config.yaml` you'd match all _files_.

The third piece to the puzzle is the defaults provided by the manifest:

[from here](
https://github.com/prettier/prettier/blob/974b27dac25be0f8e6d368f3d511dccaaa4b282e/.pre-commit-hooks.yaml#L6):

```yaml
    # From https://github.com/prettier/prettier/blob/133303f47a30f6b3e46ffdf9d5c2d6609d65c416/src/options.js#L32-L42
    files: \.(css|less|scss|html|ts|tsx|graphql|gql|json|js|jsx)$
```

It's likely that this regex hasn't been updated as `prettier` has learned how to work on other file types.  Probably worth a PR at the very least?

prettier originally used `types`, but it supports a wide variety of things and so it was switched to using `files` -- here's the relevant issue : https://github.com/prettier/prettier/issues/2745

Putting it together, your manifest as written is asking for:

- filenames that match `\.(css|less|scss|html|ts|tsx|graphql|gql|json|js|jsx)$`
- **AND** files that are markdown
- **AND** files that are scss

So an empty set.

You may also be interested in this issue, though there are no current plans or ideas for implementation: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/607

Thanks for the issue -- hope this was helpful.

Also looks like there's a test that needs `'verbose': False` here: 

https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/7f0b427b7475796d9eb861744aeb68601238a5d5/tests/repository_test.py#L791-L792

Would be pretty easy.  The problem with yaml rewriting is it's hard to make it retain the formatting as seen in https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/210 and https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/pull/211

The runtime order is also sometimes important (like I'd rather run autopep8 before flake8, whitespace fixers before anything else, etc. etc.).  But yeah I could see some people wanting alphabetized so it seems like a good idea for a hook.

The biggest problem is backporting bug-fixes from master to the latest release and LTS release.
Let's assume we now have 3.1 as latest, the master branch is for upcoming 3.2, LTS is not defined yet but following my logic it should be 2.X (2.3.Y as the latest release for 2.3 line).

Let's assume PR-XXX from master should be backported to 3.1 and PR-YYY is very critical, it should be backported to 3.1 and 2.X as well.

How should the procedure be?

In CPython we manage it by adding github labels to PR: "need backport to 3.6" and "need backport to 2.7".
On PR merging a smart bot creates two PRs with cherry-picking already squashed-and-merged PR into `3.6` and `2.7` branch. If the bot fails it reports by messages in parent PR with ask for creating these PRs by hand.
I love this bot, hope to setup something similar for aiohttp but now we have no such tool.

As the first step we can add github labels and ask PR author for making a PR for backporting. Now I do backporting myself but it is boring.
The contributor should have instructions for backport PR making. I suggest adding a section to https://docs.aiohttp.org/en/stable/contributing.html for backporting procedure. We can add a link to the section when asking for backport PR.

P.S.
aiohttp 3.X has a strong commitment to not break existing *public* API in next 3.Y releases (but deprecate the API parts if needed).
It is not documented properly yet but pronounced on this issue tracker only.
What is a *public API* is a good question, I assume it is *documented* API. We can stabilize our *public API* vision during aiohttp 3.X life cycle.
aiohttp 4.0 has a freedom for dropping all deprecations except the latest release (as Django do).

Thoughts?

> If it doesn't know about that session, it errors. I am wondering if, before erroring, it could check the on-disk cache of running kernels (e.g., in ~/Library/Jupyter/runtime). If it find the kernel_id in question, it would start a new session connected to that existing kernel. To say it another way, I see that the cache of kernels known to the manager includes only those initiated by that particular server process, not any kernels started by some other processes.

Many people inadvertently start many servers as they think "Oh jupyter-notebook just open the browser" falling back on reading files will create weird conditions when multiple server connect to same kernel, same file hence why we don't do it. 

If we go this route I would *suggest* that the server write a file saying I'm this PID , on this host I "own" the file. And once another server do the file lookup, it check whether the said process is still running.



> Would that be a crazy thing to do? The goal is to enable something like ipython console --existing <kernel_id> in the notebook server.

We had a quick discussion with Fernando some time back. We think that `jupyter console --existing` should bring a UI (in general, if is tty) that allow user to select console. Regardless of whether it's ran from the server. 

> For example, suppose Alice and Bob are running separate notebook servers on the same machine. Alice wants to allow Bob to observe the inputs and outputs in a console or notebook that she is working on. She sends Bob the connection file info (perhaps via a hub extension). Bob opens a console and creates a new session connecting to that running kernel. Now Alice and Bob each have a session connected to the same kernel. Since the JupyterLab console now mirrors iopub messages from any session, not just the session that initiated execution, Bob can see Alice's inputs and outputs. He can also execute code. This is one simple mode of real-time collaboration.

I'm sure almost no-one will understand the security risk of that. *If* bob connect from another session if should likely get a connexion though some other kind of connexion (Fake kernel connexion infos ?) that make it clear he is not Alice.  In general I think a better (and more complicated) way of having collaboration would be for Alice and bob server to make a handshake, and for Bob server to see remote kernels "exposed" by Alice server. 

Also Alice and Bob likely don't have the same signature key for messages, so should sharing kernel.json files requires Alice to give bob access to sensitive info ?

There is also similar to a need for handover where the user wants to share a subset of the data.

Requirement: Share project but exclude intermediate files and scripts.
For specifying what to download/share I am planning on adding two mutually exclusive flags: `--include <pattern>` and `--exclude <pattern>`. Where _pattern_ is a comma separated list of absolute paths.

To handover just the folder named _rawdata/_ of a project a user could do this:
```
ddsclient handover -p mouse --user dcl9 --include rawdata/
```
Or to exclude a _scripts/_ folder from a handover a user could do this:
```
ddsclient handover -p mouse --user dcl9 --exclude scripts/
```

Download would work similarly:
```
ddsclient download -p mouse --include scripts/
```

```bash
cd /usr/local/

cd /usr/local/galaxy
```

Then install all the requirements for Galaxy:

So it looks like there is an emerging consensus.  When constructing a new configuration:
  1. Start by looking for the port 443 configuration.  If it exists, use it as the model.
  2. If there is no port 443 configuration, use the port 80 configuration if it exists.
  3. Otherwise complain that construction is not possible.

By way of contrast, the "tried and failed" scenario is not worth worrying about.  The main counterargument invokes the time-honored principle:  Garbage in, garbage out.  If the user had a syntactically incorrect configuration before calling letsencrypt-auto, why should anybody expect a correct configuration afterwards?

#### Suggestion:

It seems that conceptually there are three phases of operation.  Ideally they would be separate top-level commands:
* Certonly -- just obtain a certificate, plain and simple.
  * This can be done using a stand-alone temporary server, in which case the prior server configuration (if any) will be ignored.
  * Alternatively, this can be done using an existing working server.
* Preconfigure -- Using the old configuration as a model, this constructs a new proposed-configuration that points to the new certificate ... but leaves the old configuration undisturbed.  
  * This can be done using a standalone temporary server, but nevertheless it requires a workable old configuration, obviously.
  * Alternatively, this can be done using an existing working server.
* Install -- This replaces the old configuration with the newly constructed configuration.

#### Rationale:

To answer the question about why someone would choose certonly (or certonly + preconfigure): 

I take security seriously.  I take web server uptime seriously.  I don't have a "spare" web site to play with.  There is no way I am going to turn a piece of "beta" software loose to automatically munge the configuration on any site I care about.  I need to know exactly what's going to be done, before it gets done.

To say the same thing another way, letsencrypt needs some sort of --dry-run mode, so that users can be confident of what's going to be done, before it gets done.  Many, many system-administration tools have such a mode.  A stated goal of the project is good ***usability***.  Instilling confidence is part of the basic usability concept.

@fillest _UNKONWN_TOPIC is an internal error code for librdkafka that indicates an operation tried to use a topic that librdkafka doesn't know about. The message wouldn't even have been sent out at this point, so you'd need to retry it.

In general, retries will be applied except for permanent errors, so given your very large # of retries and the default backoff time of 100ms, you should generally only see errors if there is a sustained problem with your cluster. Regarding the individual errors:

* TRANSPORT: Indicates connections to the cluster can't even be made (as `No route to host`) would suggest. This is not even tied to an individual request.
* NODE_UPDATE: Indicates the hostname changed (e.g. maybe you moved a broker ID to a new host). This shouldn't cause any problems -- the old connection will be torn down and a new one initiated.
* REQUEST_TIMED_OUT: In an on_delivery, this means the data could not be written by the broker in the timeout specified in the produce request (i.e. the `request.timeout.ms` configuration)
* NOT_ENOUGH_REPLICAS: the message was completely rejected by the broker and has not been written at all because there aren't enough in sync replicas to satisfy the request.
* NOT_ENOUGH_REPLICAS_AFTER_APPEND: this means that the broker accepted the message and wrote it to it's own log because when the record reached the broker there were enough in sync replicas, but then one more more of the replicas disappeared and never replicated the data. In this case the data is written, but there is a risk of loss because it has been written to fewer replicas than requested. Of course, it also might be fine if the other replica came back up and continued replicating data again.
* MSG_TIMED_OUT: Indicates that it took too long to be processed (it may be in flight or may still just be buffered locally). The timeout for this is specified by `message.timeout.ms`.

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, missing, fields
from marshmallow.decorators import POST_DUMP

class BaseSchema(Schema):

    def __init__(self, set_default=missing, set_missing=missing,
                 clear_dump_missing=False, *args, **kwargs):
        self.set_default = set_default
        self.set_missing = set_missing
        self.clear_dump_missing = clear_dump_missing
        super(BaseSchema, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def on_bind_field(self, field_name, field_obj):
        if (self.set_default is not missing and
                field_obj.default is missing):
            if self.set_default is None:
                field_obj.allow_none = True
            field_obj.default = self.set_default
        if (self.set_missing is not missing and
                field_obj.missing is missing):
            if self.set_missing is None:
                field_obj.allow_none = True
            field_obj.missing = self.set_missing

    def _invoke_dump_processors(self, tag_name, data,
                                many, original_data=None):
        if self.clear_dump_missing:
            if many:
                data = [self.clear_missing(d) for d in data]
            else:
                data = self.clear_missing(data)
        return super(BaseSchema, self)._invoke_dump_processors(
            tag_name, data, many, original_data
        )

    def clear_missing(self, data):
        if not self.clear_dump_missing:
            return data
        result = data.copy()
        for key in filter(lambda key: data[key] is None, data):
            del result[key]
        return result
```

The set of constructs definitely diverges from this initial proposal. Furthermore, the names are different: there are SMT-LIB2 naming conventions currently used in CVC4 that we parse as well: for example, it is called str.len instead of seq-length. Z3 lets you form sequences over arbitrary sorts, not just characters, so in fact str.len is the name that may be used if the argument is a string, and more generally we use seq.len for sequences. Thus, there are str.len, str.at, str.contains, str.++ to be friendly for the useful special cases of strings and there is seq.len, seq.at, seq.contains, seq.++, etc to use more reasonable names according to what is really supported. A basic procedure is now checked in, but requires tuning before I would say it is ready for creating applications around. At the very least it gives users some way to formulate constraints over strings natively. I will update the online tutorial on rise4fun.com with syntax and examples when it is better tuned.

From a grammatical point of view, I think it makes sense: a qualified observation expression is still an observation expression.  All observation expressions may be qualified. Those addresses are in libc! We have no simprocedure for gets since there's no good way to emulate it symbolically, since the effect can cover literally the entire memory space. Or at least, that was the answer a year ago, since then we've implemented some options for the maximum amount of symbolic data a write can deal with normally. We should look into writing a good gets simprocedure.

So in some sense this is correctly emulating the binary, there is a literal infinite state explosion inside the libc binary.

Note - you can detect blocks that are split like this by examining `block.jumpkind` - it will be `"Ijk_NoDecode"` when there's been a decoding error.

There's no easy way to get the block to continue across unknown instructions besides adding an extension as fish described. You could probably just get away with modeling them as NOPs for a first pass.

there's another problem with the ganga dirac ui and that is that the
certificates directory is not being updated. You will need some kind of
cron job to keep this and the crls up to scratch otherwise it'll stop
working very soon.
While you could argue that dirac-proxy-init should not return 0 if there is
an error (but it still makes a proxy), the instructions for using it all
include the fact that you need to source the dirac version of bashrc, in
which  case the command works as advertised.
Pretty much anything on the grid will need X509_VOMS_DIR setup, so I don't
understand how ganga gets away without it.
The instructions on the GridPP wiki should work independently of whether
you have a grid ui installed or not, but they won't work either if you
haven't sourced bashrc unless you setup X509_VOMS_DIR by hand (or in your
login script).

Hi @dordure 
At the moment we don't plan to expand our CI and validation infrastructures to additional Linux distributions. We are still working on supporting all the different flavors of Ubuntu 16 and adding Ubuntu 17.  
In practice, this should not prevent you from using RealSense on your system, and we will do our best to help. 

There are two main ways to enable RealSense on any Linux-based system:
1. Follow the steps in [installation.md](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/installation.md) to build the SDK from source and apply necessary kernel patches. Check what is your kernel version (using `uname -r`) - for the SR300 you should be able to avoid kernel patches starting from kernel 4.12. 
1. Build the SDK from source with CMake flag `-DFORCE_LIBUVC=true`. This will bypass Video4Linux subsystem eliminating the need to patch the kernel. This comes at some expense of stability since everything that is usually done in the driver will now be performed in user-space.

If I understand correctly, essentially what you're trying to do here is to convert an owned, held reference into an unowned, unheld reference—essentially to turn a regular object into one that would have been created had it been returned with `return_value_policy::reference`.  There are, of course, some dangers involved in doing that (e.g. if a Python instance stays around past the point where the pointer becomes invalid), but that's just the same danger you'd get with `rvp::reference` in the fist place.

Some comments for fixing this up:

- In addition to releasing the holder, you should invoke its destructor, and call `set_holder_constructed(false)` on the `value_and_holder`.  (That call isn't in `master` yet, but will be soon as part of #805).
- You need to set `owned = false` on the instance so that the pointed-to memory doesn't get deallocated when the Python variable gets deallocated.
- Related to that, we're now going to be in a situation where `owned` applies at the pointer level rather than the `instance` level.  (Currently that can't happen because the only way to get `owned = false` is through a cast, but a cast can only ever give you a single-pointer instance so this isn't an issue).  With Python-side multiple inheritance, this would result in the consumed pointer being unowned while other, unconsumed pointers in the instance (for the other C++ types) are still owned.  #805 adds status bits for non-simple layouts, so moving this into `value_and_holder` (and renaming `owned` to `simple_owned` for the simple holder layout) would probably be fairly easy.
- I think it makes more sense to propagate this feature through the `py::arg` mechanism rather than needing to specify the argument number.  Thus you would do something like:
    ```C++
    m.def("consume", [](int a, Type *b, Type2 *c) { ... },
          py::arg(), py::arg(), py::arg().disown());
    ```
    to indicate that the argument should be converted to unowned before calling the function (if not already disowned).  (And of course, you could add keyword names, e.g. `py::arg("c").disown()`).
- You'll have to deal with the `unique_ptr` assumption, and also the assumption that `.release()` will work.  Adding a static method to `holder_helper` would probably work (it could also have a `static_assert` fallback for holder types without a `release()` so that attempting to use the feature on a type without a `.release()` wouldn't allow the feature to be specified).

Another idea worth thinking about:
- it might be nice for symmetry to be able to go the other way, i.e. to reclaim ownership after the call (so, essentially, to convert a `reference` into a `take_ownership`), via something like `py::arg().take_ownership()`.

We could move this into `ClientV2` if we wanted, but this approach saves us the extra round trip and prevents us from changing the account on disk from ACMEv1 to ACMEv2.

On initial sync, the messages are received, but the room listeners weren't added yet (as the rooms weren't even created yet), so they aren't handled. So, at the end of `start`, all messages in the queue (sent while the node was offline) would have been received, but discarded. After start, only new messages/messages sent **after** initial sync, may be received and should be handled (well, actually after the respective `start_health_check` calls were made too).
#2081 proposes using matrix to have an on-server cursor of received messages, and sending them only one at a time, waiting for the read receipt to send another. This should ensure there's at most one message in the queue, and that they'll be handled once the node comes back online, so no need for retries.

The problem running as `task` is that I can no longer keep some "log info" of jobs I run inside the notebook. Tasks is great for running many jobs, but not very friendly to simple case. For example, all I want is to run some `plink` pipeline for QC, yet have my notebook show all those stderr from plink so that I can keep track of number of samples / variants at each step, and save the info for future review. If something really need to be reused, defining them as proper Python modules makes more sense to me.

Came across an article which is somewhat related to the discussion here: http://250bpm.com/blog:71

The first part defines "structured concurrency". Trio already strictly enforces this property (except the pass-nursery-to-child trick which is clearly documented to violate it -- the author of the article also describes a way to violate the property, by analogy to stack vs. heap) so this is already familiar.

The second part discusses cancellation. The author chose to go with "exiting scope cancels all tasks with a graceful cancellation mechanism" rather than Trio's "exiting scope waits for all tasks" approach. Though this might just be due to the Author's starting point (golang-inspired) or their choice of programming language (C).

Notes:

* [PEP 568](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0568/) provides one possible path for making cancel scopes in (async) generators "just work"; discussing this is a todo for python 3.8.

* There's some discussion that's relevant to this issue starting at https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/265#issuecomment-370509223

@1st1: I suspect that this isn't the ideal implementation, but to demonstrate that my idea can be precisely specified: for parsing, we could parse just like we do now, and then do a post-processing pass over the AST where we replace all terms of the form `Await(value=Call(...))` with a term of the form `AwaitCall(...)`. I believe that gives exactly the semantics I'd propose. And a possible bytecode implementation would be to add a bytecode `RESOLVE_AWAITCALL` which does (excuse the horrible pseudocode, hopefully the intent is clear):
```python
tmp = POP()
try:
    PUSH(tmp.__acall__)
except AttributeError:
    PUSH(tmp)
```
and then `AwaitCall(...)` compiles into the same bytecode as `Call(...)` would, except with a `RESOLVE_AWAITCALL` inserted at the appropriate place.

@dabeaz
> Honestly, forgetting to put an `await` on things is not something that I've lost a lot of sleep thinking about.

Out of curiosity, since I know you do a fair amount of training, have you done any async/await training yet? Part of the trigger for this thread was that when I wrote the trio tutorial aiming at teaching folks who hadn't been exposed to async/await before, I felt obliged to put in a big section up front warning them about this pitfall, since my impression is that it bites basically everyone repeatedly when they're learning. Also it doesn't help that in pypy the warning is delayed for an arbitrarily long time. (In particular, if you're at the REPL you probably just won't get a warning at all).

>  There are parts of Curio that instantiate coroutines and pass them around to other locations to be awaited later. So, I certainly wouldn't want to see a requirement that forces the await to be there.

From [this comment](https://github.com/dabeaz/curio/issues/122#issuecomment-284043189), I was under the impression that curio *users* would no longer be instantiating coroutines and passing them around. Is that right?

If curio internally wants to do that, then of course that's fine – any proposal is going to have to have *some* way to get at a coroutine object, or we could never start our coroutine runners at all :-). The idea is just that since this is something that only coroutine runners need to do, it should have a different spelling that users won't hit by accident.

>  I'd also add that with decorators and feature such as `functools.partial()` it gets pretty complicated.

Ugh, that's a great point. I guess in any proposal, wrappers like `functools.partial()` would need some explicit strategy to pass on the calling context to the wrapped function. In the `__acall__` approach I can see how this would work in principle, though it's a bit cumbersome: partial objects would need an `__acall__` method that defers to the wrapped object's `__acall__` method. @1st1, in the frame introspection approach, how would you handle `functools.partial`?

> I'd take a second opinion on this. Several projects like ipyparallel, boto, and even the C Programming Language book start with a beginners section followed by the reference section. 

I think we're in complete agreement! I do not think the class documentation should be a part of the userguide. I think it should be its own section labelled 'Reference guide' or something like that. 
Currently they are hidden under `Developer documentation -> Developer documentation` such that we're basically saying: 'user, this is not relevant for you.' But often a non-beginner user just wants a fast place they can go to just see what all of the options are (they do not want to read through all of the explanations because they already know them, they just want to look it up.) This is why I think it makes sense to have the class documentation in its own section, _without_ any additional verbage, such that some can quickly find the signature for a given class. Something like:

1. Quickstart
2. User guide (includes overview, detailed descriptions of various components, FAQ)
3. Reference guide (this is what I'm proposing adding)
4. Developer documentation

Having a separate reference guide is the standard on most projects I have used. See [matplotlib](https://matplotlib.org/contents.html), [ROOT](https://root.cern.ch/doc/master/modules.html) for example.

`Why wouldn't the output of BlockAggregate not have unique entries by identity?`

I may be doing some wrong assumptions here, please correct me if so:

It's not that it __won't__ have unique entries. They will be unique, because _I wrote the aggregate that way_. It's about whether the DTC can  **guarantee** that they will be unique.

For the tutorial example:

- the Streaming DTC defines `user_id` as Identity
- The Streaming DTC produces session aggregates
-  session aggregates are time-aggregated by the Window DTC. 
- Because `user_id` is the Identity in the Streaming DTC, it is **guaranteed** that in the Window aggregation `user_id` is unique per row. 

For the example I posted before, in order to use the aggregation result as features, we **must** have unique `user_id`. In the tutorial example, the result of the Streaming DTC **does not** have unique values for the `user_id` identity. 

Is there something to play with Identity, so I can guarantee the resulted transformation can be used as features, with unique `user_id`?

Right now my feeling is reusing our work at conda-forge to do this is the best path. We will have more resource generally (compute, manpower, storage, etc.) to push this forward. Not to mention we can easily store the wheels on anaconda.org right along side our conda packages.

Expect the wheel folks will be interested in smoothing out the compatibility issues once they see some demonstrated success of wheels in conda-forge. Perhaps by making manylinux2 for instance or broadening the manylinux spec to include arbitrary glibc. There's already some pressure on them to do this as tensorflow and other packages are breaking the manylinux spec anyways ( https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/issues/8802 ). Also [CentOS 5 is an unsupported OS as of last year]( https://wiki.centos.org/FAQ/General#head-fe8a0be91ee3e7dea812e8694491e1dde5b75e6d ). So they will have to update and they know this ( https://github.com/pypa/manylinux/issues/96 ).

The technical issues of getting wheels in there are minimal IMHO. Will have to see how others in conda-forge feel about having wheels included.
